result:
On Tuesdays the museum opens its doors to the hush that happens between a cup and its saucer, the soft hesitation right before porcelain meets porcelain. People think silence is the absence of sound. We collect what refuses to be cataloged as noise or music. We collect the little slips, the wanderings. There is no admission fee. There is, however, a promise you make at the front desk: please listen for what you did not know you were missing.

I have been the curator here for eight years. My predecessor retired to a town with a river that speaks only at night. She left me a handwritten guide, a narrow book stitched with green thread. In it are the rules: never uncork more than one sorrow at a time. Label laughter by season, not volume. If a visitor is weeping in Gallery Five, let them. The room will change shape around them and by closing time it will be back to its usual dimension.

I carry my keys on a ribbon. Each key opens one of the glass cabinets or the staff stairwell or, once, an accordion I found in the alley. The keys keep switching teeth, as if conspiring to open something I haven’t yet discovered. The museum is housed in the old telegraph office. In the foyer you can still see the brass hooks where messages once hung in loops, disciplined and urgent. Now those hooks hold umbrellas wet with rain we have not yet heard fall.

In Gallery One we keep the exhalations that follow a held breath. There are a hundred jars here, each a different species: the breath of children right after a magician reveals the secret, the breath of a chorus before the conductor lowers their hands, the breath of a diver at the surface counting backward. I rotate them weekly. The labels are written in pencil because the line between relief and resignation is finer than pen ink can respect.

A man in a brown coat comes every Thursday looking for a laugh he once lost under a chair in 1973. He does not say so; he pretends to browse. I do not ask questions I cannot answer. But I see how he leans toward the thin tin of late-summer laughter, how he smiles and then the smile turns cautious, like a cup being set down without a saucer. There is no guarantee here. We do not claim to return. We only arrange.

The Sound Garden, so named by a volunteer who cannot keep a plant alive but loves the idea of tending, is an open-air courtyard where long pieces of wire tremble in the breeze and sing a pitch that seems to come from a place your body recognizes before your mind approves. Children run fingers along the wires and call them harp-grass. The sound of dawn in the courtyard is different from dawn on the street. It is the difference between a known road and a known road in a dream.

We have difficult days. Sometimes a group arrives, all matching lanyards, a conference of consultants hired by a donor who wants measurable outcomes. “How do you track impact?” they ask. I gesture to the visitor book, where someone has drawn a cloud and written beneath it: “I thought I had lost this.” They frown. Impact, I suggest, is what you hear for weeks after the train has passed. They nod, write it down, and I can tell it will become a bullet point in a slide deck with a blue gradient.

Once, a physicist visited and asked to listen to a rumor. I led him to a drawer where we keep the sounds that travel too quickly to belong to mouths. He leaned in, his ear almost touching the faded velvet. He said, “It’s like static, but curved.” He stayed until closing and helped me re-shelve misfiled apologies, which look like moths and must be coaxed into the right box. For weeks after, the museum had the clean smell of a lab that has been used for a purpose other than the one it was built for.

If you’ve never heard the sound of a book deciding not to be written, I recommend Room Seven. It’s a long room with a high skylight, and the afternoon light kneels there in the most respectful way. The unbooks nest in porcelain bowls shaped like ears. When you listen close, you can hear retreating footsteps, a kettle not quite boiling, a page inhaling and then folding around a thought as it leaves. There is one bowl that holds the sound of a sentence that was too kind to be said aloud. I keep it close to the door, so that kind enters and exits with each person who does not know they need it.

We are often given donations without provenance. Someone will arrive with a shoebox, speaking in the tone of a person who has decided to be brave at least for the duration of this errand. Inside: the last hinge-sigh of the door to a house that’s been sold; the brief drum of a dog’s nails on a kitchen floor no one will walk across again; the sound a city makes when its traffic lights all, by mistake, turn green at once. I log these in the ledger, make the best labels I can. The ledger is cloth-bound, a reliable blue. I have, quietly, taken to annotating it with pencil sketches of the donors’ hands.

When I first took the job, I tried to return sounds to their owners. I called numbers, wrote letters, posted notices: To the woman who lost a hum in the summer of 1999, to the man who left behind the echo of his first yes—please come collect. Few did. Those who came were often surprised by what they collected. One woman came for her grandfather’s chair and left with the sound of her own name, mispronounced with care by a teacher who loved her enough to keep trying. We learned, after a while, to let the museum do its rearranging. If a sound wants to be found, it will stand up on its own little legs and be obvious.

Of course there are dangers. You cannot carry despair through the stairwell; it echoes and doubles. You cannot open more than two jars of orchestral tuning at once; they argue and leave the instruments offended for days. Once, a boy, small and fierce, sprinted through the corridor where we keep the unstruck matches. He flung open a case and for a second the hallway bloomed with every birthday that had ever been interrupted. We turned off the lights and sat on the floor until the hallway settled. The boy’s mother apologized. I said what I always say: it’s not a library. It’s a field.

At closing, the building exhales. I lock the cabinets, turn each sign to Resting, pull the covers over the more fragile collections. There is a sound I let out into the city at dusk, a habit I cannot explain except by saying that the museum takes and the museum keeps and something in me insists that it also give. I release it from the back step where the ivy holds the bricks together in a way mortar cannot. It varies. Tonight I choose the small, bright click of two people realizing at the same moment that they are thinking the same thought. It flits down the alley and, I hope, up into a window where someone needs it.

It rains. You might ask what rain sounds like here, as opposed to outside. In the courtyard the wires sing and the gutters applaud. In Room Seven the bowls hum like far-off bees. In the foyer, the brass hooks glisten. I stand under the skylight and listen to all the unbooks changing their minds. The city outside runs on rails and hurry; in here, we hold a different timetable, made of pauses and near-misses. Sometimes a visitor will stand in front of an empty case and say, “I hear nothing.” And I will say, “Yes. Listen to how it’s shaped.”

I do not know what this place will be in ten years. Perhaps the donors will insist on interactive exhibits you can photograph yourself inside of. Perhaps the building will develop a new set of stairs while I am at lunch and I will come back to find a landing that was not there before, leading to a room that keeps the sound made when a letter arrives three days earlier than expected. Perhaps I will retire to the river town and teach the current to keep secrets.

For now I keep the keys. For now, on Tuesday mornings, I unlock the front door and let in the hush between cup and saucer, and I set the day in motion with a sweep of my hand like a conductor, and the museum opens its ears. People step out of the rain, shake off their weather, read the promise on the wall, and begin.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
